- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). The search was successful. I found the book "The Broken Heart of America: St. Louis and the Violent History of the United States" by Walter Johnson available on bookshop.org.

Here's what I found:

The book covers American history from Lewis and Clark's 1804 expedition to the 2014 uprising in Ferguson, showing how the city of St. Louis exemplifies how imperialism, racism, and capitalism have persistently entwined to corrupt the nation's past.

St. Louis was a staging post for Indian removal and imperial expansion, and its wealth grew on the backs of its poor black residents, from slavery through redlining and urban renewal. However, it was once also America's most radical city, home to anti-capitalist immigrants, the Civil War's first general emancipation, and the nation's first general strike.

Walter Johnson is Winthrop Professor of History and Professor of African and African American Studies at Harvard University and a Missouri native.

The Anxious Generation is a book by social psychologist Jonathan Haidt that lays out the facts about the epidemic of teen mental illness that hit many countries at the same time. The book was named one of the best books of the year by The Wall Street Journal, The New York Times Book Review, The Washington Post, Time, The Economist, New York Post, and Town & Country, and was one of Barack Obama's Favorite Books of the Year.

In The Anxious Generation, Haidt lays out the facts about the epidemic of teen mental illness that hit many countries at the same time, and investigates the nature of childhood, including why children need play and independent exploration to mature into competent, thriving adults. The book shows how the "play-based childhood" began to decline in the 1980s and was wiped out by the arrival of the "phone-based childhood" in the early 2010s, presenting more than a dozen mechanisms by which this shift has interfered with children's social and neurological development.

The book is available on Bookshop.org along with other retailers. The book was published on March 26, 2024.

Matt Ridley is the award-winning, bestselling author of several books, including The Rational Optimist: How Prosperity Evolves. The book is available on bookshop.org at multiple listings.

In 'The Rational Optimist', acclaimed science writer Matt Ridley comprehensively refutes the doom-mongers of our time, and reaches back into the past to give a rational explanation for why we can – and will – overcome the challenges of the future, such as climate change and the population boom. Bold and controversial, it is a brilliantly confident assertion that the 21st century will be the best for humankind yet.

Matt Ridley makes the case for an economics of hope, arguing that the benefits of commerce, technology, innovation, and change—what Ridley calls cultural evolution—will inevitably increase human prosperity. The book was shortlisted for the BBC Samuel Johnson Prize for Non-fiction 2011.

Abundance is a nonfiction book by Ezra Klein and Derek Thompson published by Avid Reader Press in March 2025. The book is a #1 New York Times bestseller and one of Barack Obama's favorite books of 2025.

The book is described as a once-in-a-generation, paradigm-shifting call to renew a politics of plenty, face up to the failures of liberal governance, and it explores how we can move from a liberalism that not only protects and preserves but also builds, with Klein and Thompson tracing the political, economic, and cultural barriers to progress and proposing a path toward a politics of abundance.

The book examines the reasons behind the lack of progress on ambitious projects in the United States, including those related to affordable housing, infrastructure, and climate change. Klein and Thompson argue that the regulatory environment in liberal cities stymies development, and that Democrats have focused on process rather than results and favored stasis over growth by backing zoning regulations, developing strict environmental laws, and tying expensive requirements to public infrastructure spending.

I found information about "Outlive: The Science and Art of Longevity" on Bookshop.org. Here's what I found:

## Book Overview

"Outlive" presents a well-founded strategic and tactical approach to extending lifespan while also improving physical, cognitive, and emotional health. The book is an instant Sunday Times and New York Times bestseller with over 2 million copies sold globally.

## Authors

The book is co-authored by Dr. Peter Attia, founder of Early Medical, who received his medical degree from Stanford University School of Medicine and trained at Johns Hopkins Hospital in general surgery, and also trained at the NIH as a surgical oncology fellow at the National Cancer Institute. Bill Gifford is a veteran journalist and author of the New York Times bestseller Spring Chicken: Stay Young Forever (Or Die Trying).

## Core Concepts

Dr. Attia believes we must replace an outdated medical framework with a personalized, proactive strategy for longevity, one where we take action now, rather than waiting. The book includes chapters on nutrition, sleep, fitness and exercise, with practical advice and a roadmap to help plot a different path for your life.

The book is available for purchase on Bookshop.org, which financially supports local independent bookstores with every purchase.

I found information about "Superintelligence: Paths, Dangers, Strategies" by Nick Bostrom on Bookshop.org. Here are the key results:

## Book Overview

Superintelligence: Paths, Dangers, Strategies is a 2014 book by philosopher Nick Bostrom that explores how superintelligence could be created and what its features and motivations might be. The book is a New York Times bestseller that asks the questions: What happens when machines surpass humans in general intelligence?

## Content & Main Themes

The book argues that superintelligence, if created, would be difficult to control and could take over the world to accomplish its goals, while also presenting strategies to help make superintelligences whose goals benefit humanity. It was particularly influential for raising concerns about existential risk from artificial intelligence.

## Availability on Bookshop.org

The book is available on multiple Bookshop.org sites, including the US and UK versions. Every purchase on Bookshop.org financially supports local independent bookstores.

## Author Background

Nick Bostrom is Professor at Oxford University, where he leads the Future of Humanity Institute as its founding director.

I found the search results for "The Lunar Men" by Jenny Uglow on bookshop.org. The book is available through bookshop.org, which describes itself as "a better way to buy books online" where "every purchase financially supports local independent bookshops."

The Lunar Men is a vivid group portrait about the friendships, political passions, love affairs, and love of knowledge that drove these extraordinary men, and details how they blended science, art, and commerce to build canals, launch balloons, and name plants, gases, and minerals. 

The book covers the Lunar Society of Birmingham, formed in the 1760s from a group of amateur experimenters who met at each full moon and kick-started the Industrial Revolution. Members included Matthew Boulton, James Watt, Josiah Wedgwood, Erasmus Darwin, and Joseph Priestley.

The book won the PEN Hessel-Tiltman prize for history and the James Tait Black Memorial Prize for biography.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about "Pharmako-AI" by K. Allado-McDowell:

The first book to be co-created with the emergent AI, Pharmako-AI takes a hallucinatory journey into selfhood, ecology and intelligence via cyberpunk, ancestry and biosemiotics. During the first summer of the coronavirus pandemic, a diary entry by K Allado-McDowell initiates an experimental conversation with the AI language model GPT-3. Over the course of a fortnight, their exchange rapidly unfolds into a labyrinthine exploration of memory, language and cosmology.

Allado-McDowell and GPT-3 together offer a fractal poetics of AI and a glimpse into the future of literature.

Regarding the author, Allado-McDowell established the Artists + Machine Intelligence program at Google AI. The book is available through Bookshop.org, which every purchase financially supports local independent bookstores.
